title: More Ways into Insert
subtitle: Five doors into Insert mode — pick the one that lands the cursor where you want.
summary: i and a are the two basic doors into Insert mode. I, A, o, O are the convenience doors that combine a motion with the entry.

blocks.text: {key:i} inserts *before* the cursor. {key:a} appends *after*. That covers most of life — but Vim gives you four more doors that do a useful little motion on the way in.

blocks.label: Insert at first non-blank of line

blocks.caption: A — append at end of line

blocks.text: Notice the pattern: the lowercase letter does the obvious thing at the cursor; the uppercase letter does the *bigger* version (start/end of line, or line above/below).

blocks.title: These Are Just Motions + i
blocks.text:
Internally, {key:I} is exactly {key:^}{key:i} — jump to first non-blank, then enter Insert mode. {key:A} is {key:$}{key:a}. {key:o} and {key:O} create a new line (respecting `'autoindent'` and `'indentexpr'`) and drop you into Insert mode at column 0 of it.

This matters because Vim doesn't really have six "insert commands." It has *one* (the mode transition), composed with motions you already know. Once you internalize that, the keyboard stops feeling arbitrary.
All six are *changes*. {key:.} repeats whatever you typed in Insert mode (along with the entry command). So {key:o}-type-a-line, then {key:.}, opens another line below and types the same line again.

rows: Last place you left Insert mode
